name: PocketFlow
tagline: A 100-line minimalist LLM framework with zero dependencies
metaDescription: PocketFlow is a 100-line, zero-dependency Python LLM framework built on a simple graph abstraction. Build agents, workflows, RAG, and multi-agent apps with no vendor lock-in.
overview: PocketFlow is a deliberately tiny LLM framework written in about 100 lines of Python. It ships with zero extra dependencies and no vendor-specific wrappers, so there is no bloat and no lock-in to a single model provider.

The whole framework is built around one idea: a graph. Nodes do the work and connect to each other, and from that single abstraction you can express common patterns like agents, workflows, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).

Because the core is so small, the project leans into "agentic coding" — using AI assistants such as Cursor to help you build your own agents on top of the framework. There are also community ports for TypeScript, Java, C++, Go, Rust, and PHP.
features: Tiny core: the entire framework is roughly 100 lines of Python with zero dependencies and zero vendor wrappers
Graph-based design that lets you compose nodes into flows for any LLM application
Supports popular patterns out of the box: agents, multi-agents, workflows, and RAG
Model-agnostic — you wire in whichever LLM and tools you want, with no built-in lock-in
Large cookbook of runnable examples, from a basic chat bot to map-reduce, text-to-SQL, MCP, and voice chat
Ports available in TypeScript, Java, C++, Go, Rust, and PHP

gettingStarted.intro: PocketFlow is published on PyPI. You can install it with pip, or simply copy the 100-line source file straight into your project.

steps.heading: Install PocketFlow
steps.body: Add the package to your Python environment with pip. Alternatively, you can copy the single 100-line source file from the repository instead of installing anything.

steps.heading: Learn the patterns from the cookbook
steps.body: The repository includes a cookbook of small, runnable examples covering chat, structured output, workflows, agents, and RAG. Browsing these is the fastest way to see how nodes and flows fit together before building your own.

useCases: Building LLM agents and multi-agent systems without pulling in a heavy framework
Prototyping workflows and RAG pipelines using a small, easy-to-read core you can fully understand
Teaching or learning how LLM frameworks work by reading the entire 100-line graph abstraction
Using AI coding assistants to scaffold custom agents on top of a minimal, dependency-free base
